title: How Do Large Teams Build Software? Breaking Problems into Black Boxes
description: Students use functions, abstraction, and module interfaces to design app architecture that large teams can build and maintain collaboratively.

roleModelOfDay.name: Adele Goldberg
roleModelOfDay.intro: A computer scientist who co-developed Smalltalk — one of the first programming languages designed around reusable, modular components. Her ideas shaped how every modern app is built: from independent, interchangeable parts that teams can work on separately.

vocabulary.def: A named, reusable block of instructions that takes an input and produces an output
Hiding internal complexity so a user only needs to know what goes in and what comes out
An independent, self-contained component of a larger system
The data or information a function receives to do its job
The result a function produces after processing its input
A component where you can see what goes in and what comes out, but not how it works inside

learningObjectives: Explain how abstraction and functions help teams build complex software from independent components.
Describe function interfaces using clear inputs and outputs while hiding internal logic.
Design a modular architecture for a real-world app and map dependencies between functions.
Identify single points of failure and missing modules in a system design.
fullDescription: Age group: 12-17. Duration: 30 minutes. SLO alignment: Domain A (21A), Domain B (22B). Learning goal: students can explain how abstraction and functions allow large teams to build complex software through independent, reusable components, then apply this to design a food redistribution app architecture.

teacherInstructions.setup: Write vocabulary on the board and keep it visible all lesson. Prepare whiteboard space for interface diagrams and architecture maps. Have paper and pencils ready for group work.
steps: Phase 1 (0-2 min): Ask spark question about how many lines of code and engineers are behind a share action in a social app.
Phase 1 hook: compare Apollo Guidance Computer scale to modern mobile systems and explain why componentization enables both reliability and team velocity.
Phase 2 (2-8 min): Human machine demonstration with three student functions. Call by function name in different orders to show reuse and orchestration.
Phase 2 concept board: FUNCTION name, INPUT, OUTPUT, BLACK BOX. Emphasize that caller only needs contract, not internals.
Phase 3 (8-13 min): Draw black-box interface diagram and introduce team workflow where groups agree on interfaces and build internals independently.
Phase 3 failure mode: if teams disagree on input or output format, integration fails even when internal code is correct.
Phase 4 (13-24 min): App architecture challenge for surplus-food routing from supermarkets and restaurants to shelters.
Step 1 (4 min): each group defines at least five functions with explicit input and output contracts.
Step 2 (5 min): groups map function-call flow and dependencies with arrows.
Step 3 (2 min): groups identify missing function for capacity changes between claim and arrival, then integrate it.
Phase 5 (24-28 min): discussion on interface communication failures or data-governance implications of app byproduct data.
Exit reflection (28-30 min): students write when abstraction helps and when it becomes risky.
discussionPrompts: Can a system fail even when each part works locally? Give an interface-mismatch example.
NASA Mars orbiter failure involved unit mismatch across teams. What does this reveal about communication versus coding?
Who should own data generated by a social-good logistics app, and what are risks if it is sold?
If your architecture lacks delete-data or privacy modules, is that an oversight or a value choice?
tips: Keep students at interface level: function name, input, output, dependency, not implementation details.
When groups describe internals, redirect by asking what another team must know to call the function successfully.
Probe single points of failure such as login modules connected to most flows.
Reward explicit data format agreements because those are integration-critical.
Treat missing-module discovery as success, not as design failure.

teacherGuide: Core message: complex software is manageable only when teams agree on interfaces and hide internal complexity behind reusable modules. Abstraction is productive when contracts are precise and dangerous when no one owns integration quality. Use system-mapping language throughout: components, interfaces, dependencies, failure propagation.

studentExercises.title: Human Function Machine
studentExercises.description: Model functions as named black boxes to demonstrate invocation, sequencing, and reuse without knowing internal logic.
instructions: Assign three students fixed tasks as callable functions.
Invoke functions in different sequences and repeat counts.
Discuss what caller needed to know to run each function.
Identify black-box idea and interface contract.
List two everyday abstractions used in class.
successCriteria: Students explain function call behavior using names and outcomes.
Students distinguish interface from internal implementation.
Students identify abstraction in non-software systems.

studentExercises.title: Interface Contract Practice
studentExercises.description: Define robust input and output contracts for core app functions.
instructions: Write at least five function names for the food-redistribution app.
For each function, specify precise input fields and output fields.
Check whether another team could call each function from contract alone.
Revise any ambiguous input or output definitions.
Mark one contract likely to cause integration bugs if underspecified.
successCriteria: Each function has explicit input and output definitions.
Contracts are specific enough for independent implementation.
Group identifies at least one integration risk proactively.

studentExercises.title: Architecture Dependency Map
studentExercises.description: Map function dependencies, identify critical paths, and add missing modules for real-time capacity changes.
instructions: Draw arrows showing which functions call which, in order.
Trace what breaks if findNearestShelter fails.
Identify one single point of failure in your design.
Add missing function to handle shelter capacity changes after claim.
Show where the new function connects and what data it receives and returns.
successCriteria: Dependency map reflects coherent execution flow.
Group identifies at least one critical-path component.
Group patches a missing module with clear interface placement.

studentExercises.title: Exit Reflection — Power and Risk of Abstraction
studentExercises.description: Write one sentence on how abstraction helps and where it can become dangerous.
instructions: Complete: Abstraction is useful because ______, but dangerous when ______.
Include one concrete example from today's architecture challenge.
Ensure your danger clause references interface clarity or system-wide understanding.
Share strongest phrasing with class.
successCriteria: Student explains productivity benefit of abstraction.
Student identifies at least one realistic abstraction failure mode.

studentChallenges.title: Single Point of Failure Challenge
studentChallenges.description: Prioritize which functions need redundancy when resilience budgets are limited.
instructions: Identify function depended on by most other components.
Argue whether it deserves redundancy and why.
Name one function that should not get redundancy first.
State who should decide redundancy priorities in a real organization.
successCriteria: Student identifies criticality using dependency logic.
Student balances reliability gain against cost.
Student assigns decision ownership with justification.

studentChallenges.title: Data Governance Extension
studentChallenges.description: Evaluate ownership and privacy controls for data produced as a byproduct of logistics optimization.
instructions: List one sensitive byproduct dataset your app creates.
Define who can access it and for what purpose.
Add one function for delete-on-request or consent management.
Describe one harm if governance is ignored.
successCriteria: Student recognizes data byproducts as architectural concerns.
Student proposes at least one concrete governance mechanism.
Student links governance omission to practical harm.

lessonSlides.title: Get the Room Ready
whatToDo: Write these six words on the board — leave them visible all lesson.
Clear whiteboard space — you will need room for interface diagrams and architecture maps.
Have paper and pencils ready for group work.
Identify three students you can use for the human function machine demo in Phase 2.
boardWords: Function: a named, reusable block of instructions that takes an input and produces an output
Abstraction: hiding internal complexity so a user only needs to know what goes in and what comes out
Module: an independent, self-contained component of a larger system
Input: the data or information a function receives to do its job
Output: the result a function produces after processing its input
Black box: a component where you can see what goes in and what comes out, but not how it works inside

lessonSlides.title: Adele Goldberg
lessonSlides.roleModel: Adele Goldberg (born 1945) is a computer scientist who worked at Xerox PARC — the research lab that invented the graphical user interface, the mouse, and ethernet. She co-developed Smalltalk, one of the first object-oriented programming languages. Smalltalk was revolutionary because it was built around a simple idea: software should be made of independent, reusable components that communicate through clear interfaces. Each component is a black box — you tell it what to do through its interface, and it gives you a result. You do not need to know how it works inside. This idea — modular, component-based design — is the foundation of every modern app, website, and operating system. When Steve Jobs visited Xerox PARC in 1979, he saw Smalltalk running and was so impressed that it influenced the design of the Macintosh. Adele initially resisted showing the technology to Apple, recognising its commercial value. She was right — the ideas she helped create went on to shape the entire computing industry.
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Introduce Adele simply: 'Today's role model helped invent how modern software is built. Her name is Adele Goldberg. She worked at Xerox PARC — the lab that invented the mouse, the graphical interface, and the way computers look today. She co-created a programming language called Smalltalk, based on one powerful idea: build software from independent, reusable parts that communicate through clear contracts. Today, you are going to use that exact idea to design a real app.'
lessonSlides.realWorldConnection: Adele Goldberg's Smalltalk influenced Java, Python, Ruby, and Swift. Every app students use — from Instagram to Google Maps — is built from modular components that communicate through interfaces. The architecture challenge students do today uses exactly the design principles she pioneered.

lessonSlides.title: What We're Doing Today
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Welcome the class: 'Today we are going to learn how large teams build software — because no one person writes an entire app alone. You are going to learn about functions, abstraction, and black boxes. Then you are going to use them to design the architecture of a real app — one that routes surplus food from supermarkets and restaurants to shelters that need it.'
whatToDo: Point to the vocabulary on the board: 'These six words are what we're working with today. By the end you will use all of them.'

Say: 'The app you are going to design is not imaginary. Apps like this exist — Too Good To Go, OLIO, FoodCloud. They all solve the same problem: food is wasted in one place while people go hungry in another. Your job is to design the system that connects them.'
This lesson is 30 minutes. Keep the energy high and transitions sharp.

lessonSlides.title: Spark Question
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Ask the class: 'When you tap the share button on Instagram, how many lines of code do you think run behind the scenes? And how many engineers do you think worked on that one feature?'
whatToDo: Take 2–3 guesses.
Say: 'The Instagram app has millions of lines of code. Thousands of engineers work on it. But no single person understands all of it. So how does it work?'

Answer: 'Because it is built from parts — independent components that each do one job. Each component is a black box: you give it input, it gives you output. You do not need to know how it works inside. That is how large teams build software — and that is what you are going to learn today.'

Optional hook: 'The Apollo Guidance Computer that landed on the moon had about 145,000 lines of code. Your phone has billions. The only reason that is possible is because of the idea we are learning today: break the problem into parts.'
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If the class is quiet, try: 'Think about sending a message on WhatsApp. What happens? Your phone encrypts the message, sends it to a server, the server finds the recipient, delivers it, shows a tick. Each of those is a separate component. If one breaks, the message fails — but the others keep working.'

lessonSlides.title: The Human Function Machine
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'I am going to turn three of you into functions. Each of you has one job. I am the caller — I will call you by name, give you input, and you give me output. Ready?'
whatToDo: Pick three students. Give each one a simple, fixed task:
Student A — function name: DOUBLE. Input: a number. Output: the number times two. Example: DOUBLE(5) → 10.
Student B — function name: GREET. Input: a name. Output: 'Hello [name]!' Example: GREET('Sam') → 'Hello Sam!'
Student C — function name: REVERSE. Input: a word. Output: the word backwards. Example: REVERSE('cat') → 'tac'.
Stand at the front. Call functions in different orders: 'DOUBLE(7)!' Student A answers: '14.' 'GREET(Maria)!' Student B answers: 'Hello Maria!' 'REVERSE(code)!' Student C answers: 'edoc.'
Now chain them: 'GREET(REVERSE(dog))!' — Student C goes first: 'god'. Then Student B: 'Hello god!'

Ask the class: 'Did I need to know HOW they reversed the word or doubled the number? No. I only needed to know three things: the function name, what input to give, and what output to expect. That is the interface.'
Write on the board: FUNCTION = name + input → output. BLACK BOX = you see what goes in and comes out, not how it works inside.
Say: 'This is abstraction. Hiding the complexity inside and only exposing the interface. Every app on your phone works this way.'
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If students are shy, make it fun: deliberately give wrong input. 'DOUBLE(banana)!' Student A: 'I don't know what to do with that.' Say: 'Exactly! The function only works if you give it the right input. That is why contracts matter — you have to agree on what goes in.'

lessonSlides.title: Human Function Machine
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'What did the caller need to know to use each function? Name, input, output. That is the contract. That is the black box.'
whatToDo: This exercise runs during Phase 2.
After the demo, ask the class to name two everyday abstractions: a vending machine (input: money + choice → output: snack), a light switch (input: flip → output: light).
Push: 'You don't need to know how electricity works to use a light switch. That is abstraction.'

lessonSlides.title: Interface Contracts and Team Workflow
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Now let's see how this works in a real team. Imagine three teams building one app. Team A builds the login. Team B builds the search. Team C builds the notifications. How do they work together without stepping on each other's code?'
whatToDo: Draw three boxes on the board: LOGIN, SEARCH, NOTIFY.
For LOGIN, write: Input: username, password → Output: user object (name, role, permissions).

Say: 'Team B needs to call LOGIN before showing search results. But Team B does not build LOGIN — Team A does. So they agree on a contract: Team A promises that LOGIN will take username and password and return a user object. Team B writes their code trusting that contract.'
Now the failure mode: 'What if Team A changes the output format — they rename user to account — without telling Team B?'
Answer: 'Team B's code breaks. Not because Team B made a mistake, but because the contract was broken. This happens in real software all the time.'

Say: 'The NASA Mars Climate Orbiter crashed because one team used metric units and another used imperial. Each team's code worked perfectly. The interface contract was wrong. The spacecraft was destroyed.'
Close: 'Contracts are not paperwork. They are the glue that holds a system together. If the contracts are wrong, the system fails — even if every part works.'
lessonSlides.realWorldConnection: The Mars Climate Orbiter cost $327 million and was lost because of a unit mismatch between teams. Lockheed Martin sent thrust data in pound-force. NASA's navigation software expected newtons. Each team's code was correct. The interface was wrong. This is the single most famous example of why interface contracts matter.
lessonSlides.roleModelMoment: Adele Goldberg's Smalltalk introduced exactly this: each component exposes what it does, not how it does it. The interface is the contract. That principle is in every major codebase built since the 1970s.

lessonSlides.title: Architecture Challenge — Setup
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Now it is your turn. In groups of three, you are going to design the architecture for a real app: a surplus-food routing system. Supermarkets and restaurants have food they cannot sell. Shelters and food banks need it. Your app connects them.'
whatToDo: Form groups of three.
Say: 'You are not writing code. You are designing the system — the functions, their inputs and outputs, and how they connect. Think of it as the blueprint.'

Give the scenario: 'A supermarket has 50 sandwiches expiring today. A shelter 3 km away needs food for 40 people. A restaurant has leftover soup. A food bank across town has space. Your app must match supply to need — fast.'
Say: 'Step 1: define at least five functions your app needs. Step 2: map how they connect. Step 3: find what is missing.'

lessonSlides.title: Define Your Functions
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Step 1: write at least five functions your app needs. For each one, give the function name, input, and output. Be specific — another team should be able to build it from your contract alone.'
whatToDo: Walk around. Push for specificity.
Common functions groups will define: listSurplusFood, findNearestShelter, claimFood, routeDriver, notifyShelter.

If a group writes 'match food to shelter' as one function, push: 'How does that function work? Does it need to know where shelters are? Does it need to know what food is available? That is probably two or three functions, not one.'
Push for precise inputs: 'findNearestShelter needs a location. What format? GPS coordinates? Postcode? City name? Be specific — the contract must be clear.'
Push for precise outputs: 'claimFood — what does it return? A confirmation? A pickup time? A driver assignment? Specify it.'
After 4 minutes, ask one group to share their function names. Class comments.
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If groups struggle to start, give a seed: 'Every app starts with a login. Function: authenticateUser. Input: email, password. Output: user profile with role (donor, driver, shelter). Now — what function comes next?'

lessonSlides.title: Interface Contract Practice
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Write at least five function names with precise input and output contracts. Could another team build each function from your contract alone?'
rules: At least five functions — each with a clear name.
Each function has specific input fields and output fields.
Contracts must be precise enough for independent implementation.
Mark one contract that could cause integration bugs if underspecified.
whatToDo: This exercise runs during Phase 4 Step 1.
See the Phase 4 Step 1 slide for full facilitation instructions.

lessonSlides.title: Map the Dependencies
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Step 2: draw your architecture map. Show which functions call which — the data flow through your system. Use arrows.'
whatToDo: Walk around. Ask key questions:
'Which function runs first? What triggers it?'
'Which function depends on the output of another? Draw an arrow.'
'What happens if findNearestShelter fails? What breaks downstream?'

Push groups to trace failure: 'If the driver notification function fails, does the shelter still expect food? What happens to the donor's inventory? A failure in one component can cascade through the system.'
After dependency maps are drawn, ask each group: 'Which function is your single point of failure — the one that, if it breaks, takes down the most other functions?'
Most groups will identify login or findNearestShelter as critical.
Say: 'A single point of failure is a design risk. Real systems build redundancy for these components — backup servers, fallback logic.'
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If groups draw flat lists instead of maps, push: 'You drew five functions. But which ones talk to each other? Does notifyShelter need output from claimFood? Draw the connection. If there is no connection, your system has no flow.'
lessonSlides.roleModelMoment: Goldberg saw this early: when modules depend on each other's internals, one change cascades and breaks everything. Your dependency map shows precisely why her abstraction principle still holds.

lessonSlides.title: Find the Missing Module
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Final step. Here is the problem you did not anticipate: a shelter claims 50 sandwiches. A driver is dispatched. But while the driver is en route, the shelter fills up — another delivery arrived first. Your system does not know. What happens?'
whatToDo: Let the problem sink in.
Say: 'Your system needs a function you did not design: one that checks or updates shelter capacity in real time — between claim and arrival.'
Give groups 2 minutes to define this missing function: name, input, output, where it connects.
Example: checkCapacity — Input: shelterId, claimedQuantity → Output: confirmed/rejected, remainingCapacity.
Say: 'Finding a gap in your design is not failure. It is exactly what real architects do — they test their system with scenarios and patch the gaps before they become bugs.'
Close: 'Adele Goldberg designed Smalltalk to make these gaps visible. A modular system lets you add a new component without rewriting everything else. That is the power of abstraction.'

lessonSlides.title: Architecture Dependency Map
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Map your function dependencies. Trace failures. Find your single point of failure. Then add the missing module.'
whatToDo: This exercise spans Phase 4 Steps 2 and 3.
See the Phase 4 Step 2 and Step 3 slides for full facilitation instructions.

lessonSlides.title: Discussion
whatToDo: Pick the question below that feels most alive based on how the class went.
One strong discussion is better than two rushed ones.
Give students a moment to think before taking answers.
discussionQuestions: 'Can a system fail even when every single part works correctly?' Follow-up: 'The Mars orbiter crashed because both teams' code was correct — but they used different units. That is an interface failure, not a code failure. Where could that happen in your food app?'
'Your food-routing app generates data as a byproduct: which shelters need food most, when donors have surplus, driver routes. Who owns that data? What happens if someone sells it?'
'Your architecture does not have a delete-data function or a consent-management module. Is that an oversight — or a choice? What are the consequences?'
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If discussion is slow, try: 'Imagine a food company sponsors your app. They want access to the data — which neighbourhoods are food-insecure, what shelters are under-resourced. Is that helpful or exploitative? Can it be both?'

lessonSlides.title: Exit Reflection — Power and Risk of Abstraction
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Final task. On your own, complete this sentence: Abstraction is useful because ______, but dangerous when ______.'

lessonSlides.title: Close the Lesson
whatToDo: Ask every student to write and complete the sentence on paper or sticky note.
They must include one concrete example from today's architecture challenge.
The danger clause must reference interface clarity or system-wide understanding.
Collect papers or stick them on the board.
Read 2–3 strong responses aloud.
lessonSlides.exitReflection: Abstraction is useful because ______, but dangerous when ______.
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Close with: 'Adele Goldberg proved that the hardest part of building software is not writing the code — it is agreeing on how the parts connect. You just experienced that. Next lesson, you will learn about a different kind of structure — one that decides what gets processed first, and why that decision matters more than most people think.'
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If reflections are shallow, push: 'Do not just say abstraction helps with big projects. Be specific. What did abstraction let you do today that you could not do without it? And what went wrong — or almost went wrong — because of it?'
